qa_pairs.question: Where can I find information about the differences between StdEnv/2023 and earlier environments?
qa_pairs.answer: The differences are discussed in the "Standard software environments" document.

qa_pairs.question: When will StdEnv/2023 become the default environment for all clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: StdEnv/2023 will become the default environment for all clusters after April 1, 2024.

qa_pairs.question: Can I customize my default standard environment?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, you can specify your own default environment at any time by modifying the `$HOME/.modulerc` file.

qa_pairs.question: How do I change my default standard environment to StdEnv/2020?
qa_pairs.answer: To change your default standard environment to StdEnv/2020, run the command `echo "module-version StdEnv/2020 default" >> $HOME/.modulerc`.

qa_pairs.question: What action is required for a change to my default environment to take effect?
qa_pairs.answer: You must log out and log in again for the change to your default environment to take effect.

qa_pairs.question: What file should I modify to set my default standard environment?
qa_pairs.answer: You should modify the `$HOME/.modulerc` file to set your default standard environment.

qa_pairs.question: Is it necessary to recompile or reinstall my code when the StdEnv changes?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, if you compile your own code or have installed R or Python packages, you should recompile your code or reinstall the packages with the newest version of the standard environment.

qa_pairs.question: What should I do with my R or Python packages when the StdEnv changes?
qa_pairs.answer: You should reinstall your R or Python packages with the newest version of the standard environment.

qa_pairs.question: How can I continue using an earlier environment for an existing workflow?
qa_pairs.answer: To continue using an earlier environment for an existing workflow, add `module load StdEnv/2020` to your job scripts before loading any other modules.

qa_pairs.question: Where should I add `module load StdEnv/2020` if I want to use an earlier environment?
qa_pairs.answer: You should add `module load StdEnv/2020` to your job scripts before loading any other modules if you want to use an earlier environment.

qa_pairs.question: Will older standard environments be removed?
qa_pairs.answer: No, earlier environments and any software dependent on them will remain available.

qa_pairs.question: Which specific older standard environment versions are no longer supported?
qa_pairs.answer: Versions 2016.4 and 2018.3 are no longer supported.

qa_pairs.question: Is it recommended to use unsupported earlier standard environment versions like 2016.4 or 2018.3?
qa_pairs.answer: No, it is not recommended to use unsupported earlier environments like 2016.4 and 2018.3.

qa_pairs.question: In which environment will staff install new software?
qa_pairs.answer: Staff will only install software in the new environment 2023.

qa_pairs.question: Is it permissible to mix modules from various environments within a single job?
qa_pairs.answer: No, you should use a single environment for a given job.

qa_pairs.question: What are the potential consequences of mixing modules from different environments in a single job?
qa_pairs.answer: The results of trying to mix different environments are unpredictable and generally lead to errors of one kind or another.

qa_pairs.question: Can different jobs use different standard environments?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, different jobs can use different standard environments by explicitly loading one or the other at the job's beginning.

qa_pairs.question: Which standard environment is recommended for new projects?
qa_pairs.answer: If you are starting a new project, you should use StdEnv/2023.

qa_pairs.question: How do I specify that I want to use StdEnv/2023 for a project?
qa_pairs.answer: You should add `module load StdEnv/2023` to your job scripts.

qa_pairs.question: Do I need to remove `module load StdEnv/2023` from my job scripts after April 1?
qa_pairs.answer: No, this command does not need to be deleted to use StdEnv/2023 after April 1.

qa_pairs.question: What environment should I use if I want a newer version of an application?
qa_pairs.answer: You should use StdEnv/2023 if you want a newer version of an application.

qa_pairs.question: Is loading modules in my `.bashrc` file a recommended practice for using an older environment?
qa_pairs.answer: No, loading modules in your `.bashrc` is not recommended.

qa_pairs.question: What is the recommended method for loading modules instead of using `.bashrc`?
qa_pairs.answer: Instead of using `.bashrc`, you should explicitly load modules in your job scripts.

qa_pairs.question: Does this change to standard environments apply if I only use cloud resources and not HPC clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: No, this change will only affect the available software accessed by using environment modules, not cloud resources.

qa_pairs.question: What aspects are affected by the standard environment changes?
qa_pairs.answer: The changes only affect available software accessed by using environment modules.

qa_pairs.question: Why might I no longer be able to load a module that I previously used?
qa_pairs.answer: More recent versions of most applications are installed in the new environment, which may be why a module you previously used is no longer loadable.

qa_pairs.question: How can I check the available versions of modules?
qa_pairs.answer: To see the available versions of modules, run the `module avail` command.

qa_pairs.question: What command can I use to check available versions of the GCC compiler?
qa_pairs.answer: You can use `module avail gcc` to see available versions of the GCC compilers.

qa_pairs.question: Are GCC compiler versions necessarily the same in new and older environments?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the GCC compiler versions in the new environment may be different from those in earlier environments.
